In the video "15 Insane CSE Projects...", it was suggested to create a web development project that combines JavaScript with neural networks. The idea is to build a game in JavaScript and have a bot learn to play it, with the goal of beating the high score. The recommended approach was to use the JavaScript canvas for the game.

I am considering a similar concept but on a larger scale. My plan is to develop a 2D open-world pixel game where NPCs and vehicles are controlled by AI bots. Each bot would have a specific goal and attempt to achieve it, improving its performance over time. For this purpose, I am leaning towards using Phaser.js. From my observation, Phaser.js is more suitable for scaling up complex projects, whereas working directly with the canvas tends to make the code longer and more difficult to manage.

But what's your take on this?

It all boils down to batch updates in react ..

Without going into detail
For both the chess board and the element box I used flex display
The chess board uses the border collapse property for maintaining the 8x8 square
(chessboard was by chatgpt)
The box uses flex direction column and the buttons are under another div with a different justice content than the parent div
The nav bar is default in .NET (what I use), it basically uses bootstrap
The change in color is by the game logic that I wrote using outline, and basic background color change
Pretty basic stuff

this visualization is either very stupid or genius. Either way it must have been hard to code. My guess is that they are extracting the spectogram of the audio to make this. But since there every point is represented as a vector of 3, they might be extracting multiple features from the audio like amplitude and banded frequency perhaps. They are taking all this to eventually reduce the whole song into a 3d vector using PCA. Most likely they just named the resulting dimensions as "tonality" and "spectral centroid." Lots of cool engineering but they failed to make a good looking visualizer that gives any insight about the audio, but i cud be missing some fata

"it's just the design part", that's the exact excuse you can use to go deep end on those nitty gritty topics, that's what i would call as a "mock project/product" and really learn a ton and lots of lots of details on how this entire design connects to the bigger picture. Okay?

if u would fix your design eye, you would notice design patterns everywhere, that design lens stays with you as you continue moving away and doing entirely different things, and that helps, a ton, I am physicist, and AI/ML enthusiast, trust me, it helps me on a daily basis, like I know what are the right things to look out for, and thereby I know what questions should I ask. This is really just 1 example, u can find a lot if u keep up with this habit
